text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
Attending: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Patient returns for follow-up and restaging evaluation after completing 3 cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap) following curative-intent pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 67-year-old woman of Ghanaian descent with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head, originally diagnosed approximately 14 months ago. Her treatment course has been extensive and is summarized here for context.

She initially presented with a 10-week history of painless jaundice, 14-pound weight loss, epigastric pain radiating to the back, and steatorrhea. Workup revealed a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process with SMA abutment over approximately 180 degrees and SMV narrowing, consistent with borderline resectable disease by AHPBA criteria. CA 19-9 was elevated at 1,204 U/mL at diagnosis. EUS-guided biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

She underwent biliary decompression via ERCP with fully covered metallic stent placement, followed by neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 [reduced], leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 continuous infusion) for 8 cycles. Due to progression of peripheral neuropathy to grade 3 after cycle 6, oxaliplatin was discontinued and cycles 7-8 were completed as FOLFIRI. She achieved a radiographic partial response with the mass shrinking from 3.8 cm to 2.6 cm and significant reduction in SMA abutment, and CA 19-9 declined to 85 U/mL.

She subsequently received consolidation chemoradiation -- IMRT with SIB (54 Gy in 28 fractions) concurrent with capecitabine 825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days -- which she tolerated reasonably well with manageable nausea, mild dermatitis, and transient diarrhea. Post-chemoradiation restaging showed the mass at 2.3 cm with SMA abutment less than 60 degrees, CA 19-9 52 U/mL, and no distant disease.

She then underwent open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with en bloc SMV segmental resection and primary reconstruction. Final pathology demonstrated a 2.1 cm moderately differentiated PDAC with CAP tumor regression grade 2, R0 resection (SMA margin 1.2 mm, neck margin 3 mm), 1 of 22 positive lymph nodes, perineural invasion present, LVI absent. Pathologic stage ypT3N1M0 (Stage IIB). Comprehensive genomic profiling on the resected specimen identified KRAS p.G12V, TP53 p.R175H, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A/p16 homozygous deletion; MSS, low TMB; BRCA1/2 wild-type on both somatic and germline testing.

The postoperative course was complicated by a Grade B pancreatic fistula requiring drain maintenance, NPO/TPN, and octreotide. She was discharged on postoperative day 14 with a Jackson-Pratt drain, which was removed at 6 weeks after sinogram confirmed closure.

At approximately 10 weeks post-surgery (first post-operative oncology visit), her CA 19-9 had normalized to 18 U/mL, CEA 2.4 ng/mL, ECOG PS 1. She had developed new exocrine pancreatic insufficiency managed with pancrelipase (PERT), and her pre-existing type 2 diabetes had worsened post-pancreatectomy, now requiring metformin and basal insulin glargine with endocrinology co-management. We initiated adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap) per ESPAC-4 evidence, with capecitabine started at 80% dose given prior chemoradiation and post-surgical debilitation.

Adjuvant GemCap cycles 1-3 were complicated by a Grade A pancreatic fistula recurrence detected by rising drain output on cycle 1 day 10, which resolved with conservative management (clear liquids, drain irrigation, octreotide). Gemcitabine was held for 2 weeks during this episode. She developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome) from capecitabine requiring dose reduction to 75% (approximately 1247 mg/m2/day) and initiation of topical urea-based emollient cream. Myelosuppression was significant: grade 2 thrombocytopenia with nadir platelets of 68,000/mcL and grade 2 anemia with hemoglobin nadir 8.9 g/dL, necessitating day 15 gemcitabine dose omission during cycles 2 and 3. Grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 nausea were also present throughout.

She now returns for assessment after completing cycle 3 of 6 planned cycles.

Today, Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports persistent fatigue, which she rates as moderate -- she is able to perform most activities of daily living but has markedly reduced stamina compared to her pre-illness baseline. She describes some ongoing mild tingling in the fingertips, which she attributes to prior oxaliplatin and notes is unchanged from her last visit. She has mild tenderness over the palmar surfaces of her hands, better over the past two weeks since starting the urea cream, but still noticeable. Nausea is mild and generally controlled with ondansetron as needed. She denies any new pain, abdominal cramping, fever, or chills. She reports improved appetite and has gained approximately 2 pounds since her last visit. Stools are formed; she continues pancrelipase with meals without issues. She has not noted any jaundice, dark urine, or right upper quadrant pain. She expresses significant treatment fatigue and asked again today about whether all 6 cycles are truly necessary.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Moderate fatigue. Improved appetite, 2-pound weight gain. No fevers, night sweats, or chills.
Neurologic: Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in bilateral fingertips (stable, not worsening). No new neurologic symptoms.
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea controlled with ondansetron. No vomiting. Formed stools with PERT. No diarrhea, melena, or hematochezia. No abdominal pain or distension. No signs of drain output recurrence (drain removed).
Dermatologic: Persistent mild palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (grade 1-2), improving with dose reduction and topical urea cream. No blistering or skin breakdown.
Musculoskeletal: Fatigue-related reduced activity tolerance. No bone pain.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, dyspnea, or lower extremity edema.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Blood glucose management ongoing with basal insulin and metformin; per patient, glucose readings have been in acceptable range.
Psychosocial: Patient expresses treatment fatigue and concern about continuing chemotherapy but remains engaged in shared decision-making.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (carried forward)
- Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma -- as described above; status post neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (6 cycles) + FOLFIRI (2 cycles), consolidation chemoradiation (IMRT 54 Gy/28 fx with concurrent capecitabine), open PPPD with SMV resection; now on adjuvant GemCap
- Postoperative pancreatic fistula (Grade B, then Grade A recurrence during adjuvant therapy) -- resolved
- Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency -- new post-pancreatectomy, on PERT
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled initially, now worsened post-pancreatectomy, on metformin and insulin glargine (basal)
- Hypertension -- on antihypertensive therapy
- Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (predominantly oxaliplatin-related, residual grade 1)
- 15 pack-year smoking history (quit 10 years prior)

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired librarian. Lives with her spouse. Nonsmoker (quit 10 years ago, 15 pack-year history). Denies alcohol use. Support system is strong; adult children involved in care. Volunteer work at local library partially resumed prior to adjuvant therapy. Currently on medical leave from volunteer activities.

FAMILY HISTORY
No family history of pancreatic cancer. No known familial cancer syndromes. Germline BRCA1/2 testing performed and confirmed negative.

ALLERGIES
NKDA. No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. Gemcitabine 750 mg/m2 IV days 1, 8, 15 of 28-day cycle (current dose: 75% of 1000 mg/m2 standard, dose-reduced for myelosuppression) -- HOLD pending today's labs
2. Capecitabine (Xeloda) 1247 mg/m2/day orally in two divided doses, days 1-21 (75% of original 80% starting dose) -- HOLD pending today's labs
3. Ondansetron (Zofran) 8 mg orally as needed for nausea, every 8 hours
4. Pancrelipase (Creon) 72,000 units lipase with each meal, 36,000 units with snacks
5. Metformin 500 mg orally twice daily
6. Insulin glargine (Lantus) -- dose per endocrinology
7. Urea 40% cream to palms and soles twice daily
8. Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily (hypertension)
9. Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily (hypertension)
10. Aspirin 81 mg orally daily
11. Multivitamin daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert and oriented, in no acute distress. Appears chronically fatigued but comfortable. Well-nourished; weight 61 kg (gained approximately 1 kg from last visit).
Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, Temperature 36.8°C, SpO2 98% on room air.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No scleral icterus. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis today.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or crackles.
Abdomen: Midline surgical scar well-healed without erythema or induration. Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds normoactive. Drain site (right flank) fully healed, no drainage.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. Bilateral lower extremity skin intact.
Dermatologic: Bilateral palmar surfaces with mild erythema and skin thickening, consistent with grade 1 PPE, improved from grade 2 at last visit. No blistering or ulceration. Soles similarly affected but less prominently.
Neurologic: Intact cranial nerves. Diminished light touch sensation at bilateral fingertips -- consistent with grade 1 peripheral neuropathy. Gait normal. No ataxia.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC 4.1 x10^9/L (normal range 4.0-10.0)
- ANC 2.3 x10^9/L (adequate)
- Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (down from 10.2 at last visit; nadir was 8.9 in cycle 2)
- MCV 88 fL
- Platelets 112,000/mcL (recovered from nadir of 68,000/mcL in cycle 2; grade 1 at this level)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 3.9 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L, BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL (normal)
- AST 32 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L (all normal)
- Albumin 3.4 g/dL (low-normal; improved from 3.1 at diagnosis)
- HbA1c (drawn today): 7.2% (improved from 7.9% at prior visit; per endocrinology, acceptable)

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 22 U/mL (within normal limits; stable from 18 U/mL post-surgery and 21 U/mL at cycle 1)
- CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (within normal limits; stable)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging was obtained at this visit. Post-operative surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis (obtained at completion of adjuvant GemCap) is planned. The most recent imaging prior to initiation of adjuvant chemotherapy (CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast, obtained approximately 8 months post-surgery) demonstrated no evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease, with expected post-operative changes in the surgical bed, no focal hepatic lesions, no adenopathy, and no peritoneal implants.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 67-year-old woman with ypT3N1M0 pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, status post R0 pylorus-preserving Whipple procedure (with SMV resection) following neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX/FOLFIRI and chemoradiation. She has completed 3 of 6 planned cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine. She is tolerating therapy with moderate toxicity, including grade 2 myelosuppression (recovering), resolved Grade A pancreatic fistula recurrence, and grade 1-2 hand-foot syndrome (improving with dose reduction and topical therapy). Tumor markers remain in the normal range, which is reassuring.

1. ADJUVANT GEMCITABINE / CAPECITABINE (GemCap) -- Cycle 4 Planning
   - Today's CBC shows platelet count of 112,000/mcL (grade 1 thrombocytopenia) and hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (grade 1-2 anemia). ANC is 2.3 x10^9/L (adequate for treatment).
   - Given platelet recovery and adequate ANC, we will proceed with cycle 4 administration. Gemcitabine will continue at 75% dose (750 mg/m2) given cumulative myelosuppression from cycles 1-3. Capecitabine will continue at 75% dose (1247 mg/m2/day) given resolved but prior grade 2 PPE.
   - Day 1 of cycle 4 to proceed today with gemcitabine IV. Patient will resume capecitabine starting tomorrow morning.
   - Day 8 and day 15 gemcitabine will be contingent on CBC at those visits; if platelets fall below 75,000/mcL or ANC below 1.0 x10^9/L, doses will be held.
   - G-CSF (filgrastim) is not routinely prescribed for this regimen, but given recurrent myelosuppression, we discussed starting pegfilgrastim prophylaxis with cycle 4. Patient agreed; Neulasta 6 mg subcutaneous will be administered approximately 24 hours after day 1 gemcitabine each cycle going forward.
   - Iron studies will be ordered to evaluate for iron-deficiency contribution to anemia. If iron-deficient, oral iron supplementation will be initiated. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents are not routinely used in the adjuvant setting and will not be pursued.

2. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (Palmar-Plantar Erythrodysesthesia)
   - Improved to grade 1 from grade 2 with dose reduction and topical urea 40% cream. Continue current management.
   - Counseled patient to avoid tight footwear, prolonged standing, and pressure-inducing activities. Continue vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) 50 mg daily as supportive measure.
   - If PPE worsens back to grade 2-3, capecitabine will be held until resolution to grade 0-1 and resumed at 50% dose.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY
   - Grade 1 sensory neuropathy in fingertips, stable. Primarily attributed to prior oxaliplatin with possible additive contribution from capecitabine.
   - No dose modification required for neuropathy at this time. Will continue to monitor at each visit. If progression to grade 2 interfering with ADLs, capecitabine dose reduction or discontinuation will be considered.

4. PANCREATIC FISTULA HISTORY
   - Grade A recurrence during cycle 1 has fully resolved. Patient has no drain in place. No signs or symptoms of recurrence today (no abdominal pain, no fever, no palpable fluid collection).
   - No further monitoring needed beyond clinical assessment at visits.

5. EXOCRINE PANCREATIC INSUFFICIENCY
   - Continuing pancrelipase 72,000 units lipase with meals. Patient reports formed stools without symptoms of steatorrhea. No adjustment needed at this time.

6. POST-PANCREATECTOMY DIABETES MANAGEMENT
   - HbA1c improved to 7.2% with current regimen of metformin plus basal insulin glargine. Blood glucose logs reviewed and acceptable. Continue endocrinology co-management. No oncology-directed changes today.

7. TUMOR MARKER SURVEILLANCE
   - CA 19-9 22 U/mL -- stable, within normal limits. CEA 2.1 ng/mL -- stable, within normal limits. No concerning trend.
   - Will continue monitoring tumor markers with each cycle. A rising CA 19-9 in the setting of completed or ongoing adjuvant therapy warrants prompt cross-sectional imaging evaluation.

8. RESTAGING IMAGING
   - Per our surveillance plan, CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast will be obtained at completion of all 6 cycles of adjuvant GemCap (anticipated in approximately 3 months from today). CT chest will also be obtained at that time as part of standard post-Whipple surveillance.

9. PATIENT GOALS AND QUALITY OF LIFE
   - Patient expressed significant treatment fatigue and reiterated her concern about continuing chemotherapy for the remaining 3 cycles. We had an in-depth discussion about the rationale for completing all 6 cycles of adjuvant GemCap based on the ESPAC-4 data, which demonstrated improved overall survival with GemCap versus gemcitabine alone (median OS 28.0 vs. 25.5 months, HR 0.82) in resected pancreatic cancer. We acknowledged that she has had a difficult and cumulative treatment burden -- neoadjuvant chemotherapy, chemoradiation, major surgery, postoperative fistula, and now adjuvant chemotherapy.
   - We discussed the option of early discontinuation of adjuvant therapy if toxicity becomes unacceptable, and reassured her that completing cycle 4-6 at dose-reduced levels is still clinically meaningful. The patient, after discussion, agreed to continue with the plan for cycles 4-6 at current dose levels, with the caveat that if cumulative toxicity worsens significantly, we will revisit this decision.
   - A referral to oncology-based supportive care/palliative care for symptom management and quality of life support was placed and the patient accepted.
   - Social work consultation has been in place; patient's adult children continue to provide strong support at home.

10. FOLLOW-UP
    - Return to clinic in 2 weeks for day 8 gemcitabine of cycle 4 with CBC check.
    - Subsequent full follow-up visit at start of cycle 5.
    - Labs (CBC, CMP, CA 19-9, CEA) at each visit.

Time spent with patient in counseling and shared decision-making: approximately 40 minutes.

Electronically signed by:
Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
